Basic steps and exercises are used to acquaint the student with proper rhythms and styles of Mexican footwork, some based in Flamenco. Discussions of costumes and accessories pertinent to the dances are also included. Folklorico or flamenco shoes are required. Performances are once per semester.

title: Principles of Microeconomics
description: This course first develops simple graphical and mathematical models of decision-making by individual economic agents: consumers, workers, and businesses. We analyze interactions between these agents in product and factor markets using concepts of market demand, supply, and equilibrium. Finally, we demonstrate the efficiency of perfectly competitive markets, describe the conditions under which that efficiency arises, and examine market failures that occur when those conditions are not met.

title: Principles of Macroeconomics
description: This course provides an introduction to macroeconomics. The first part of the course explores how GDP, inflation, unemployment, and other macroeconomic aggregates are measured in practice. The second part develops analytical models of macroeconomic performance and growth in the long run. The third part focuses on short-run (business-cycle) fluctuations and fiscal and monetary policies. Fall and Spring.

title: Microeconomic Theory
description: This course covers value and distribution, including the theory of consumer choice, the theory of the firm, the impact on allocation of resources by different market structures ranging from competition to monopoly, game theory, and general equilibrium analyses. The concepts are given operational content where possible but the emphasis is on the development of analytic tools. Fall and Spring.

title: Macroeconomic Theory
description: This course covers the measurement of output and prices, theory of economic growth, business cycle theory, fiscal policy, monetary policy. Fall and Spring.

title: Microeconomic Theory (Honors)
description: The Honors section of Microeconomic Theory covers the topics presented in ECON-101. The subject-matter, however, is approached in a more rigorous manner. The Honors section is designed for Economics majors who intend to do graduate work in the field or for those who are interested in a more mathematical treatment than that provided in ECON-101.

title: Macroeconomic Theory (Honors)
description: The Honors section of Macroeconomic Theory covers the topics presented in ECON-102. The subject-matter, however, is approached in a more rigorous manner. The Honors section is designed for Economics majors who intend to do graduate work in the field or for those who are interested in a more mathematical treatment than that provided ECON-102. Spring.

title: Economic Statistics
description: After overviewing descriptive statistics, and the theory of probability and random variables, this course covers statistical inference in detail. Students recive the firm foundation needed for Introduction to Econometrics. Regression analysis, the primary tool for empirical work in economics, is introduced. Electronic data acquisition and computer applications receive hands-on treatment. Lab sessions meet weekly to discuss homework and the use of computer software. Fall and Spring.

title: Introduction to Econometrics
description: Prerequisites: ECON-121 and MATH-035. This course develops the theory and applications of regression analysis, which is the primary tool for empirical work in economics. Emphasis is placed on techniques for estimating economic relationships and testing economic hypotheses. Fall and Spring.

title: Economic Development
description: This course will focus on the following central questions in the field of economic development: 1. How is economic development measured and what are the development facts? 2. What causes the vast differences in economic development across countries? 3. What is the engine of growth? This course also reviews the history of development policy in regards to the goals of promoting growth and reducing poverty. Students will be required to write a paper on a topic discussed in class or to write a book report.

title: International Trade
description: This course covers the theory and practice of international trade. The first part of the course develops the classical and modern theories of the determination of the pattern of commodity trade between nations. The second part of the course covers trade policy and the role of institutions in managing world trade. Fall and Spring.

title: International Finance
description: This course deals with the theory and practice of international finance. Concepts of balance of payments and exchange rates are developed, followed by macroeconomic tools in an open economy. Balance of payments adjustments will be analyzed under fixed and flexible exchange rate systems. Macroeconomic topics, namely inflation, unemployment, and monetary and fiscal policies will be discussed in an open economy.

title: Comparative Economic Systems
description: The course analyzes the interaction between economic history and the development of the economics discipline in the first half, discussing Smith and the classical school, Keynes and his critics, and the future of capitalism as seen by Marx and Schumpeter. The second half is a brief history of the Soviet experiment with central planning and issues associated with globalization. China and Japan are also discussed as case studies.

title: Environmental Economics
description: This course uses microeconomics to analyze environmental and natural resource management issues. Topics may include market-based regulations, valuing the environment, air pollution, global warming, biodiversity conservation, fisheries, natural resource scarcity (minerals and oil), and tradeoffs between environmental quality and economic growth. Coverage of the topics will balance textbook applications of environmental economics with analysis of environmental and natural resource policy. Instruction will consist of a mix of lectures and class discussion. This course uses microeconomics to analyze environmental and natural resource management issues. Topics may include market-based regulations, valuing the environment, air pollution, global warming, biodiversity conservation, fisheries, natural resource scarcity (minerals and oil), and tradeoffs between environmental quality and economic growth. Coverage of the topics will balance textbook applications of environmental economics with analysis of environmental and natural resource policy. Instruction will consist of a mix of lectures and class discussion.

title: Tutorial: Economics
description: Tutorial.

title: Tutorial: Economics
description: Tutorial.

title: Multinational Corporations
description: The importance and distinctive nature of multinational corporations; explaining foreign investment from international trade, industrial organization, and theory of information; case studies of the impact of multi-nationals on less developed countries. Specific problems associated with multi-nationals like transfer pricing, taxation, employment and the transfer of technology to the less-developed countries. Fall and Spring.

title: Multinational Corporations
description: The importance and distinctive nature of multinational corporations; explaining foreign investment from international trade, industrial organization, and theory of information; case studies of the impact of multi-nationals on less-developed countries. Specific problems associated with multinationals like transfer pricing, taxation, employment and the transfer of technology to the less-developed countries. Graduate students may earn graduate credit for this course if they arrange to do additional work. Permission of the department is required. âRequest for Approval" forms must be obtained from and returned to the SSCE Office.

title: Economics of South Asia
description: The main objective of this course is to enable students to understand and explain the economic development performance of the countries of South Asia since independence. The countries covered include Bangladesh, India, Nepal, Pakistan and Sri Lanka. These countries are among the poorest in the world, and, therefore, the course necessarily focuses on poverty-related issues and the relationship between poverty, inequality and economic growth. The first part of the course covers the nature and causes of South Asia's development performance. In this context, the major determinants of growth are examined, including: external and domestic sector policies; macroeconomic management; human capital development; governance or public sector institutional capacity; saving rates, financial intermediation and capital accumulation; natural resources; and initial conditions. Both theoretical frameworks and empirical evidence are examined. The second part of the course is devoted to other special and related subjects including: the role of the state in South Asia's economic development; globalization and South Asia (this year's lead theme for the Human Development Report for South Asia); women and gender (last year's lead theme); the flexibility and efficiency of labor markets; the cost of conflict; official development aid; the lessons from East Asia; and South Asia's prospects for the future. Throughout the course, basic theoretical concepts and analytical tools are presented and applied to help facilitate a better understanding of the development performance of the countries in the region. Fall.

title: Economic Development of the Middle East
description: This course examines the process of economic development in the Middle East and North Africa (MENA) in the post-WWII period. It is designed to expose students to current thinking by academics and policy makers on past experience and future challenges facing the economies of the region. The emphasis will be on developing a deep overall understanding of the origins and nature of various economic problems while at the same time providing a critical examination of existing and alternative policy formulations in MENA. To this end, readings, lectures and discussions will be focused on the origins and nature of state intervention in the economy; the sources of economic growth and structural transformation; population growth and the economic impact of demographic transitions; trends in employment, poverty and inequality; the macroeconomics crisis of the 1980s and the political economy of policy reform; financial development, equity markets and Islamic banking; problems of governance and institutional development; regional trade integration and the challenge of globalization; and alternative development paths in the 21st century. Fall.

title: The Japanese Economy
description: Japan has the second largest capitalist economy in the world (third, if China is included and if its output is evaluated at purchasing power parity). Japanâs economic history has been marked by several phases since the beginning of its modernization in the 19th century: fast growth, miracle growth, deceleration, and stagnation. The main purpose of this course is to understand this history and to place it in context by relating it to the broader subject of economic development and by making analytical comparisons to other countries, especially the United States. The course has four main themes: (1) Japanese economic growth and its economic miracle in the context of economic development more generally; (2) slowdown and stagnation in the past decade; (3) transition to a more effective economic structure; and (4) other key topics such as trade, deregulation, and research and development.

title: Economic Issues in Social Security Reform
description: The objective of the course will be to improve understanding of the current Social Security reform debate. It will cover the current structure and history of the Social Security program along with the underlying macro and micro economic analysis needed to evaluate recent major reform options. In addition, the course will cover intensively the most recent developments in the reform debate, beginning with the last major legislative changes to the program in 1983, and ending with the 2001 Bush Commission on Social Security and current proposals for reform. Particular emphasis will be focused on the major economic issues underlying the political debate about "privatization". Fall.

title: Intermediate Econometrics
description: This course reviews the basics of regression analysis, and shows how it may be used in solving specific policy problems, such as estimating treatment effects. Emphasis is placed on the identification problems that commonly occur when random sampling from the population of interest is difficult or impossible.

title: Mathematics for Economics
description: This course will introduce you to the mathematical tools employed in economics. We will concentrate on optimization subject to constraints (e.g., maximization of utility subject to a budget constraint). The method of Lagrange (and generalizations by Kuhn and Tucker) will be introduced and employed to solve static problems. The methods of optimal control and dynamic programming will be used for dynamic problems.

title: Topics in Competition and Regulation
description: This course focuses on government policy towards market power broadly defined. The relevant theory will be reviewed, followed by applications. One broad set of issues involve efforts to introduce more competition into industries (such as telecom and electricity) traditionally viewed as natural monopolies: preventing a bottleneck monopolist from distorting competition in related, potentially competitive segments; dealing with cross-subsidies and alleged cream-skimming; and improving the regulation of remaining monopoly segments. The course will also address selected aspects of antitrust in unregulated industries, such as vertical control and exclusion. In addition to lectures, we will have occasional outside speakers who are experts in their fields.

title: Applied Game Theory
description: Game Theory is the study of strategic interaction among rational agents. Its core methodology is mathematical in nature. This course develops the basic tools of game theory, and builds on them to proceed to a relatively advanced level. To complement and illustrate the pure theory, the course also covers a number of economic applications. Students will be asked to complete a number of assignments available via a dedicated web-site. These assignments are compulsory, but do not form part of the final grade. Instead the final grade will be based on a mid-term and a final exam. Fall.

title: Industrial Organization
description: This course examines the behavior of business firms and the performance of markets in monopoly and imperfectly competitive settings. Aspects of strategic decision-making that will be studied include price discrimination, product selection, intellectual property, and investment decisions. Public policy toward industry (e.g., antitrust and regulation) will also be discussed, with a focus on its effect on firm behavior and consumer welfare. Game theory will be used, and case studies will be introduced to shed light on the theories. Fall.

title: Labor Economics
description: This seminar develops the basic economic theory used to model labor market behavior. Particular emphasis is placed on applying the theory to current policy issues such as welfare reform, educational policy, and social security reform. Recent developments in the labor market such as the increased labor force participation of married women with children and the decline of unionism in the United States are also discussed. A term paper is required. Spring.

title: Development Economics
description: The purpose of this course is to provide you with a basic understanding of a number of important concepts and theories in the literature on economic development. Topics to be discussed include growth, human capital, inequality, poverty and nutrition, rural credit market, tenancy and land markets. The strongest emphasis will be placed on a firm grasp of the conceptual issues involved. In addition, I will often cite data and discuss case studies. At the end of the course, I'd like you to be in a position were you can read about (or directly experience) development issues.

title: Political Economy of Trade Policy
description: The course begins with an investigation of the effects of trade policy on welfare and on the distribution of income. Next, we study trade policy in practice, examining the history of trade policy and the evolution of trade rules and institutions. This leads to an inquiry into the processes by which trade policies are formed. We examine both domestic political determinants of trade policy and models of international trade relations. Spring.

title: Monetary Economics
description: This course studies the role of money in the economy and the interaction between monetary and fiscal policy. We begin with a historical look at the evolution of money and the history of central banking, including the use of the gold standard and the current system of unbacked currency. We then build several basic models of money, using them to study the fundamental links between money, business cycles, and inflation. We use these basic principles to study the conduct of monetary and fiscal policy. One focus is on the role of inflation in helping the government finance its spending, including the tension between the central bank and the fiscal authority. We study these issues using the basic principles of optimal taxation theory. We also study alternative monetary policy instruments, including the term structure of interest rates and government debt. Throughout, we examine how the predictions of our models compare to real-world policies.

title: Economics of Health
description: The first half of this is course develops the theory of health markets, and empirical tests of the hypotheses derived from the theories. The markets include the individual's production of health, the demand for and supply of physicians' services, hospital services, and health insurance services. In the second half of the course we focus on using our knowledge developed in the first half to solve problems like what is the optimal level of health insurance and how can we alter Medicare, Medicaid and private insurance to reach optimality; how can we solve the spatial oligopoly problems developed in the hospital market in the past decade; what is the optimal number of med schools; can underdeveloped or developing countries increase their expected lives; and why is medicine so expensive? For nearly all the course, our focus is on U.S. health care markets and problems. Fall.

title: Introduction to Microtheory
description: see description for Econ 101.

title: Introduction to Macrotheory
description: See description for Econ 102.

title: International Finance
description: This course examines macroeconomics in open economies that are linked by trade, capital flows, and exchange rate policies. The goal is to provide an analytical framework for understanding current events and major challenges facing the world economy. The course will develop theoretical tools that can be used to analyze the economic performance of open economies and to evaluate alternative policy options for macroeconomic management. Topics include balance of payments accounts, foreign exchange and money markets, monetary and stabilization policies, exchange rate regimes, the capital inflows problem, currency and financial crises, and the international financial architecture. Case studies from historical and recent experiences will be closely and critically examined.

title: International Trade
description: Major topics covered include: comparative and absolute advantage; production, trade, and consumption; equilibrium in the international market; trade and factors of production; modern theories of trade; commercial policy; factor movements; U.S. trade policy; and trade problems of developing countries, current trade issues and WTO. (Not required for MSFS/MA in Economics majors).

title: International Finance
description: Major topics covered include: theories of balance of payments determination; foreign exchange markets; price and income approaches to balance of payments adjustments; monetary approach to balance of payments adjustments; Euro-currency markets; monetary integration; and the international monetary system. (Not required for MSFS/MA in Economics majors).

title: International Trade
description: Major topics covered include: comparative and absolute advantage; production, trade, and consumption; equilibrium in the international market; trade and factors of production; modern theories of trade; commercial policy; factor movements; U.S. trade policy; and trade problems of developing countries, current trade issues and WTO. (Not required for MSFS/MA in Economics majors).

title: The European Economy
description: The European Economy proceeds on a variety of levels, ranging from labor markets to foreign direct investment, trade, institutional harmonization, fiscal policy coordination and monetary union. This course concentrates on a subset of these developments: The integration of the goods market (trade) and of factor markets (foreign direct investment and labor mobility). The course follows a dual strategy, beginning with an examination of theoretical issues arising in each of the aforementioned areas, such as the determinants of trade patterns, the income distribution consequences of opening trade, the motives for capital and labor mobility, etc. These concepts are then applied specifically to the case of Europe, including the relevant economic history, the current institutional framework, the observed effects of liberalization on trade and income patterns and the remaining obstacles.

title: Microeconomics I
description: This course covers the theory of consumer and producer behavior (including comparative statics and duality), decision-making under uncertainty, expected utility, introduction to game theory, static and dynamic games of complete information.

title: Microeconomics II
description: This course examines topics in Infinitely Repeated Games, Games of Incomplete Information and associated notions of Perfect-Bayesian equilibrium and Sequential equilibrium, General Equilibrium Theory including Markets under Uncertainty, Imperfect Competition, Adverse Selection, and Moral Hazard.

title: Microeconomics IV
description: This is the second part of the Theory Field of the PhD program in Economics. Econ 601 and Econ 602 are prerequisites for the course. Econ 603 is highly recommended, but because of scheduling problems it is not a formal prerequisite for the class. Your final grade will be based on one mid-term (40%) and a final exam (60%). The complete syllabus for this class is not finalized yet. The material covered will be a combination of topics in Bounded Rationality and Contract Theory. Last, but NOT LEAST, a word of caution about MATH. This Class DOES involve some hard math. Though not all topics covered require it, the math used in some parts of the course exceeds that used in Econ 601, 602 and 603. This does not mean that I expect students to know the mathematical tools involved; we will pick them up as we go along. However, you must be willing to make some fairly heavy investments in this sense. Here now are some relevant readings. Please note that this list will be expanding as we go along. title: Macroeconomics I
description: This two-semester sequence provides an introduction to the techniques of modern macroeconomic analysis that are needed by all graduate students in economics. Intertemporal frameworks are developed to discuss modern theories of consumption, saving, investment and growth. Other applications may include theories of asset pricing, models of the labor market and search frictions, the analysis of business cycles, and fiscal and monetary policies. The sequence also provides an introduction to mathematical techniques used in dynamic macroeconomic analysis. ECON-605 is the prerequisite for ECON-606.

title: Macroeconomics II
description: This two semester sequence introduces the techniques of modern macroeconomic analysis that are needed by all graduate students in economics. A brief review of IS-LM models is followed by a discussion of the implications of rational expectations for the effectiveness of monetary policy; credibility in policy making and the Lucas-critique are also discussed. An intertemporal framework is developed to discuss modern theories of consumption, investment and growth. Fiscal policy and deficits are discussed with emphasis on intertemporal tax smoothing and Ricardian equivalence. Neo-classical growth models are developed, and endogenous growth models and real business cycle models are discussed. Basic monetary models (e.g., cash-in-advance and overlapping generations models) are also introduced. ECON-605 is the prerequisite for ECON-606.

title: Macroeconomics III
description: This course is designed for graduate students who plan to concentrate in one of the areas of macroeconomics. It develops further some of the techniques that were introduced in Macroeconomics I and II, and it discusses some of the areas of current macroeconomic research. Topics covered will vary from year to year, but likely topics include: new Keynesian models, theories of price determination, real business cycle model techniques, endogenous growth, monetary transmission mechanisms, asset pricing models, and models of banking and finance. Prerequisite: ECON-606.

title: Macroeconomics IV
description: This course is designed for graduate students who plan to concentrate in one of the areas of macroeconomics. It develops further some of the techniques that were introduced in Macroeconomics I and II, and it discusses some of the areas of current macroeconomic research. Topics covered will vary from year to year, but likely topics include: new Keynesian models, theories of price determination, real business cycle model techniques, endogenous growth, monetary transmission mechanisms, asset pricing models, and models of banking and finance. Prerequisite: ECON-607.

title: Probability and Statistics
description: This course provides the statistical foundations for econometrics and economic theory: random variables, distribution theory, estimation, and hypothesis testing.

title: Econometrics I
description: Methods of estimation and inference are developed for the classical regression model, the generalized regression model, basic time-series models, and simultaneous equations systems.

title: Econometrics II
description: This is the second econometrics course in the Ph.D. sequence focusing on nonlinear and nonparametric econometrics. Topics covered include asymptotic theory, maximum likelihood and generalized method of moments estimation, nonlinear and specification tests, and numerically methods. Students develop their own computer code to implement the latest estimation and testing methodologies. A research paper utilizing these techniques is required to complete the course.

title: Micro-Econometrics
description: Econometric methods for analysis of microeconomic behavior are developed. Topics include panel data analysis and models with discrete or limited dependent variables.

title: Macro-Econometrics
description: This course focuses on econometric methods for nonlinear models with particular emphasis on models estimated in the time domain. Topics covered include: general specification tests, non-nested testing and encompassing, the estimation of nonlinear models using conditional and unconditional moment techniques, systems estimation, and time series modeling. Emphasis is placed on understanding the intuition behind the estimation techniques and tests. Students study how recent econometric methods have been applied in the literature to address various economic issues. The course requires students to develop their own computer programs to implement the techniques and to produce an original piece of applied econometric research. Prerequisites: ECON-613 and 614.

title: Economic Development I
description: The purpose of this course is to provide you with a basic understanding of a number of important concepts and theories in the literature on economic development. Topics to be covered include: Coordination failure and history dependence; Inequality; Rural credit markets; Insurance; Savings and other protections from risk; Land; and Institution, geography and political economy. The strongest emphasis will be placed on a firm grasp of the conceptual issues involved, but empirical papers and case studies will be discussed.

title: Industrial Organization
description: The course studies theoretical models of industrial organization that are used to address competition policy and monopoly regulation. Topics covered will include some of the following: monopoly behavior and welfare losses; strategic interaction in oligopoly--prices, outputs, investments; welfare tradeoffs due to fixed costs, asymmetric costs, and product differentiation; collusion and horizontal mergers; price discrimination; vertical control--incentives and methods, including integration versus contracting, and vertical control for exclusionary purposes; promoting competition in traditionally regulated network industries; innovation and intellectual property rights.

title: International Trade
description: The subject matter of this course is trade theory and commercial policy. Topics covered include Ricardian and neo-classical trade theory; general commercial policy--tariffs, non-tariff barriers--and the political economy of policy; trade policy under domestic distortions and constraints; the economics of international integration; scale economies, imperfect competition and strategic policy; trade and growth; international factor movements; and trade and uncertainty.

title: International Trade
description: The subject matter of this course is trade theory and commercial policy. Topics covered include Ricardian and neo-classical trade theory; general commercial policy--tariffs, non-tariff barriers--and the political economy of policy; trade policy under domestic distortions and constraints; the economics of international integration; scale economies, imperfect competition and strategic policy; trade and growth; international factor movements; and trade and uncertainty.

title: Political Economy
description: Dynamic models in political economy.

title: Public Economics
description: This course studies the fundamentals of collective action. Topics include social welfare results, Arrow's Theorem, altruism and intergenerational altruism, public goods and externalities, mechanism design and implementation of collective decisions, Gibbard Satterthwaite Theorem, optimal taxation, voting behavior, legislative and bureaucratic models, social norms and the evolution of cooperation, Tiebout results and other models of migration.

title: Workshops in Macroeconomics
description: Presentations of papers by outside speakers, faculty, and students on topics in economic development, international finance, macroeconomics, monetary economics, and related fields.

title: Workshops in Macroeconomics
description: Presentations of papers by outside speakers, faculty, and students on topics in economic development, international finance, macroeconomics, monetary economics, and related fields.

title: Workshops in Microeconomics
description: Presentations of papers by outside speakers, faculty, and students on topics in industrial organization, international trade, labor economics, microeconomics, public finance, and related fields.

title: Workshop: Microeconomics
description: Presentations of papers by outside speakers, faculty, and students on topics in industrial organization, international trade, labor economics, microeconomics, public finance, and related fields.

title: Workshop in International Economcs
description: Presentations of papers by outside speakers, faculty, and students on topics in international economics and related fields.

title: Workshop: International
description: Presentations of papers by outside speakers, faculty, and students on topics in international economics and related fields.

title: Workshop:  Econometrics
description: Presentations of papers of outside speakers, faculty and students on topics in econometrics and applied economentrics.

title: Economics Tutorial
description: dissertation preparation.

title: Thesis Research-Economics
description: Thesis research and completion.

title: Gateway: Med &/or Ren Lit/Cult
description: Spring 2007 This course will introduce students to the literature and culture of medieval and early modern England, taking as our focus three areas of contention in the period: religion, race and gender. We will explore a range of developing attitudes, separately and collectively, on these controversial issues: how do criticisms of the Catholic church develop into the Reformation in England; how does the notion of female inferiority change when a queen becomes her countryâs sole ruler; how do ideas of race and ethnicity develop in a period when national identities begin to form? In order to begin finding responses to these questions, we will employ New Historicist and cultural studies approaches to help us understand the relation of literature to the period in which it is produced. We will read literary and nonliterary works from the 14th through the 17th centuries, as well as historical and theoretical essays, in order to gain a fuller sense of cultural concerns and the varying shapes they took â poems, plays, biographies, treatises, biblical commentaries, political tracts â in the period under consideration. For example, what view of women emerges when we read various church fathersâ writings on women, Chaucerâs Wife of Bathâs response to their teachings and Margery of Kempeâs account of her conflicts with contemporary church authorities? We will continue to develop and hone our skills as close readers of both poetry and prose in this course. Students will produce several short analyses of the readings and everyone will be responsible for a class presentation that will require independent research. We will learn how to find and utilize textual resources available to us in Lauinger Library and on the internet, as well as focus on conventions for citation and use of the work of others. Students will write about literary and non-literary texts of the period, as well as recent critical theory, learning to analyze language, evaluate arguments and develop their own original ideas.

title: Gateway: Med &/or Ren Lit/Cult
description: Spring 2007 Epic and Romance in English Renaissance Literature This course will focus on the genres of epic and romance in some of the most important texts of the English Renaissance. It will pay special attention to what one critic has identified as âromance episodesâ â moments at which the conflicting values of romance and epic come clearly into focus. Such conflict â between the epic heroâs dutiful prosecution of the project of nation-formation and the romance heroâs renunciation of responsibility through the pursuit of his desires â provides authors with the opportunity to address issues and themes of lasting relevance: the significance of sexuality and desire for private and public life, as well as for religious identity; the role that gender plays in constituting selfhood; the question of what it is to be human; and the place of the cultural âOtherâ in forming a personal and civic identity. The class will focus on poetry and drama by writers such as Marlowe, Shakespeare, Spenser, and Milton, as well as secondary readings on theories of epic and romance. There are no prerequisites, although some familiarity with The Iliad, The Odyssey and/or The Aeneid would be helpful. Requirements will include reading response essays, quizzes, regular attendance and participation, and papers.

title: Gateway: Med &/or Ren Lit/Cult
description: Spring 2007 Medieval Literatures and Culture This course will consider three aspects of cultural formation in medieval societies: ideology, violence, and gender. Why these three? Each of them is a prominent subject of literary study today in all periods, as is "cultural formation" itself, and all of them are important subjects of investigation in other disciplines, such as anthropology, psychology, sociology, and social history, which continue to affect, to broaden, and to interact with the study of literature. Our focus will be on two cultures in medieval Europe, which we will attempt to treat comparatively. The first is Nordic or Viking culture of the 8th to the 11th centuries, encompassing the peoples of Scandinavia, Iceland, and (after the Danish invasions) England. We will begin with Nordic mythology and religion from Snorri Sturlusson's Prose Edda: the Nordic creation story of fire and ice, the world-ash Yggdrasil, the encircling world-serpent Jormungandr, the war of the gods and the giants, Thor, Oden, and Loki. Then we will read several Icelandic sagas from the Viking period-Njals Saga, Hrafnkels Saga, and Laxdaela Saga, the first forms of prose fiction in the West- and will study the evolution of Icelandic law and feud customs, and the role of women in Nordic society. The final work from this period is the epic English poem Beowulf, where arguably literature in English begins. Reflecting the early history of England as an oft-invaded, often colonized nation, the poem, though in English, is set in Scandinavia and refracts English fears (and mythologization) of societal violence through the smoky lens of ancient Nordic history. The second culture we will focus on might be called "romance culture," in part because it is largely confined to the romance languages, but also because "literary romance," particularly Arthurian romance, is its characterizing genre. Romance culture of the 12th to the 14th centuries, like Nordic culture, was not confined within national boundaries. Hence, from France we will read ChrÃ©tien de Troyes Lancelot and Yvain, and from England, Sir Gawain and the Green Knight. We will also study the evolution of chivalric ideology as a major cultural phenomenon arising from the collapse of older feudal relations. This new ideology brings in its train at least two major formations that affected the history of Europe for 500 years: romantic love (and with it a new conception of womanhood) and knighthood (and with it a new conception of manhood). To study the new romantic love, we will read Andreas Capellanus' Art of Courtly Love. On knighthood, we will read selections from Maurice Keen's Chivalry and from the works of Georges Duby and Jacques Le Goff on the medieval "cultural imaginary." And from the end of the 14th century, we will read a classic anti-romance put ironically into the mouth of a woman: Chaucer's Wife of Bath's Tale. Finally, having seen that the term medieval has come to embrace these two widely disparate cultures, chronologically and ideologically utterly different, we will consider the origins and distortions of the terms "medieval" and "Middle Ages"- invented by a later age- and what might be called "the politics of periodization." The course will be conducted as much as possible as a seminar, with students taking shared responsibility for discussions. There will be frequent short papers and two longer papers during the semester, with an emphasis on the writing and revising process.

title: Gateway: 18th/19th Century Lit/Cult
description: Spring 2007 Disappearing Bodies, Spectral Signatures and Hidden Portraits This course explores the woman hidden and the woman hiding in nineteenth-century literature and portraiture (photograph and painting). We begin with several assumptions: obscurity provokes the observerâs desire to discover; the act of looking empowers choosing between uncovering and covering up the object of the gaze; the woman hidden by another or hiding herself reveals a multi-faceted self-consciousness. Questions weâll pose in our hide and seek: Why are women, more often than men, in hiding? What are different ways, places, and reasons women hide? How, why, and to what effect do female artists hide their subjects? How do readersâ imaginations and expectations shape female characters who challenge scrutiny? How do these constructions of female identity reflect the means and ethics of a cultureâs perception? How does gender shape attention and the gaze? What is the difference between a woman hidden and a woman who hides? Between the female existence and the female presence? What happens when a womanâs ghost or spirit appears more real than her physical self? In addition to reading about these nineteenth-century masquerading and overlooked figures, we will also study their painted and photographed portraits in our search to appreciate the Victorian allure and danger of the invisible female presence beside her equally troubling and titillating exploited image. How do posing, framing, focusing, and fashion affect the selection of what we see and what we donât? Finally, we will read some feminist theorists of both the nineteenth and twentieth century as we contemplate how the anxieties of knowledge, purity, sympathy, love, fear, guilt, beauty, and madness play a part in secreting and mystifying womenâs performance, protection, and ostracism. Required texts: Villette, Wuthering Heights, Mill on the Floss, Jude the Obscure, Lady Audleyâs Secret, Lady Windemereâs Fan, and To The Lighthouse. Critical and theoretical essays will be available through the libraryâs electronic reserve.

title: Gateway: 18th/19th Century Lit/Cult
description: Spring 2007 This course will focus on learning how to read a variety of texts from the turn of the nineteenth century. We will consider works by Robert Louis Stevenson, Henry James, Joseph Conrad, Charlotte Perkins Gilman, Arthur Conan Doyle, Edith Wharton, and others as expressions of a host of cultural anxieties â anxieties about race, class, gender, technology, and sexuality. We will develop the close reading skills required to read literature intelligently and learn the terms and concepts necessary to understand the critical discourse surrounding these texts. Be prepared for frequent writing and rewriting.

title: Gateway: 18th/19th Century Lit/Cult
description: Spring 2007 Race and Gender in Eighteenth Century British Literature The Rights of Man, Thomas Paineâs classic argument in defense of the individualâs right to assert freedom in the face of tyranny, was a popular late-eighteenth century refrain originating from the 1789 French Revolution. But generalized arguments about individual human rights also gave rise to specific debates concerning the rights of women and Negro slaves. What rights were these individuals denied in eighteenth-century Britain and how did the periodâs literature reflect their dilemmas? This course will consider these questions and others central to the study of race and gender in a British colonial context. But it is particularly concerned with occasions in literature where British writers combined simultaneous discourses about race and gender in ways that sometimes helped and at other times hindered the fights against tyranny that Negro slaves and female advocates fought. We will read plays, poetry, novels, short stories, travel literature, non-fiction prose, and a slave narrative as well as recent theories about gender and racial construction in the eighteenth century to discuss representations of British men and women, and colonial Others like Negro slaves, Creoles and the Irish. We will consider an assortment of issues ranging from slavery, anti-slavery, abolition, miscegenation, blackness and whiteness, to marriage, libertinism, and sexual double standards in a variety of canonical and obscure texts.

title: Gateway: 18th/19th Century Lit/Cult
description: The Long Eighteenth Century An introductory study of English literature of the âlongâ eighteenth-century (1660-1832), this course examines key texts produced in this period in their historical, religious, political, and cultural contexts. It serves as a âgatewayâ to more advanced electives focused on Enlightenment authors and topics.

title: Gateway:Mod &/or Post-Modern Lit
description: Spring 2007 This course introduces students to key texts in the study of twentieth-century African American literature. Stemming from the foundational treatise of W.E.B. Du Boisâs groundbreaking text The Souls of Black Folk (1903), we will consider the relationship between ârace,â representation, and narrative form; the interplay between fiction and other popular cultures; the philosophical connections between politics and poetics; alliances between configurations of the U.S. and international cultural/political movements; the creative and theoretical imprints of âmodernismâ and âpostmodernismâ; as well as those watershed moments within âcanonâ formationâliterary works which rethink both narrative form and how we come to understand the world. Authors may include: Du Bois, James Weldon Johnson, Jean Toomer, Richard Wright, Zora Neale Hurston, Ralph Ellison, James Baldwin, Toni Morrison, and Colson Whitehead.

title: Gateway:Mod &/or Post-Modern Lit
description: Spring 2007 This course introduces students to key texts in the study of twentieth-century African American literature. Stemming from the foundational treatise of W.E.B. Du Boisâs groundbreaking text The Souls of Black Folk (1903), we will consider the relationship between ârace,â representation, and narrative form; the interplay between fiction and other popular cultures; the philosophical connections between politics and poetics; alliances between configurations of the U.S. and international cultural/political movements; the creative and theoretical imprints of âmodernismâ and âpostmodernismâ; as well as those watershed moments within âcanonâ formationâliterary works which rethink both narrative form and how we come to understand the world. Authors may include: Du Bois, James Weldon Johnson, Jean Toomer, Richard Wright, Zora Neale Hurston, Ralph Ellison, James Baldwin, Toni Morrison, and Colson Whitehead.

title: Gateway:Mod &/or Post-Modern Lit
description: Spring 2007 Postmodernism and Postcolonialism This course will investigate the often tenuous and questionable relationship between postmodernism and postcolonialism by examining a number of cultural texts (literary, visual and otherwise). How are these â-ismsâ defined? How have culture and identity been shaped by the global phenomenon of colonialism and imperialism? We will consider how notions of the Empire, race, class, gender, sexuality, disability, nationality, postcoloniality, and the postmodern identity, are represented in these cultural texts and potentially destabilized.

title: Gateway:Mod &/or Post-Modern Lit
description: Spring 2007 Postmodernism and Postcolonialism This course will investigate the often tenuous and questionable relationship between postmodernism and postcolonialism by examining a number of cultural texts (literary, visual and otherwise). We will consider how notions of the Empire, race, class, gender, sexuality, disability, nationality, postcoloniality, and the postmodern identity, are represented in these cultural texts and potentially destabilized.

title: Gateway:Mod &/or Post-Modern Lit
description: Spring 2007 Popular culture is the one school of education we all share. In todayâs society, what we hear and see and participate in the mediated culture around us teaches us how to think, feel, be â even more so than do parents, church, state. We employ images from tv, ads, media of all sorts, to define our taste, style, even our identities. âYou deserve a break today!â the ad tells us. Who says?, we respond. âJust be yourself!â another ad exhorts. Yet, truth be told, we rarely do. Films we watch, commercials we see, newscasts we view â all help us make up our minds, slowly, silently, unnoticed. Think of this course as an investigation into a hidden curriculum. It is an introduction into reading culture and becoming literate in its forms. We will examine the forms of culture that are so near to us that we seldom pause to see them as forms that structure how we live and think. Why, for example, does a newspaper look the way it does? Who profits from its clear divisions into sections? Why are malls designed the way they are? Why are there no clocks in malls? We will ask questions about cinema, publishing, television, popular music â even search engines on the Internet. How do these built-in parameters âmake citizensâ â quietly coercing us to view life, and to live, by making distinctions (racial, sex/gender, ethnic, national and class) that are socially constructed and enforced? Weekly essays; mid-term and final; written portfolio; student-organized classes. Note: This course is designed for all undergraduates who will take reading / writing courses in the Humanities, as well as SFS or Business College students who wish to fulfill a General Education requirement.

title: Gateway: Mod &/or Post-Modern Lit
description: Spring 2007 This course will assist the student to continue close reading of texts with an emphasis on viewing texts from selected critical stances such as historical, psychoanalytical and mythical. We will read and discuss key texts of seven modern writers of fiction and poetryâHemingway, F. Scott Fitzgerald, Gertrude Stein, Hilda Doolittle, e e cummings, Ezra Pound and T. S. Eliot. We will discuss how the texts are distinctly Modernist in both thematic and technical innovations. Two short papers, a longer research project, occasional quizzes and a class presentation (usually with a group).

title: Gateway:Mod &/or Post-Modern Lit
description: Spring 2007 Telling Histories âHistory must be dreamed. It has to be authored. It must be turned into a fiction before it can ever be true.â In this course we will explore the seemingly paradoxical project of making history true by turning it into fiction. We will consider the ways in which modern and contemporary writers approach the problem of representing both personal and communal histories. Why is it necessary to represent history, and why is that representation so problematic? What is the relationship between narrative form and historical perspective? What does it mean to say that history has to be authored? The texts we will read will (probably) include Toni Morrisonâs Beloved, Garcia Marquezâs One Hundred Years of Solitude, Tim OâBrienâs The Things They Carried, and Art Spieglemanâs Maus. Frequent short responses papers will be required in addition to longer critical essays.

title: Gateway:Mod &/or Post-Modern Lit
description: Spring 2007 20th Century American Women Poets In this class we'll examine the writing lives of selected 20th century women poets who played significant roles in shaping the literary fields in which they worked. Specifically, we'll be studying poets who doubled as editors, critics, publishers, as well as canon- and scene-makers. We will read closely the careers of three major figures--Gwendolyn Brooks, Adrienne Rich, and Alice Notley--but we will not be limited to single authors. Rather we will broaden our sense of both literature and literary history by studying networks of cultural workers. We will have particularly rich connections to explore during the two periods in which women wielded the most power both to write and to be read:: Modernism (1910-30), and the re-emergence of women's poetic communities in the context of the feminist movements of the 70s and 80s.

title: Gateway:Mod &/or Post-Modern Lit
description: Spring 2007 20th Century American Women Poets In this class we'll examine the writing lives of selected 20th century women poets who played significant roles in shaping the literary fields in which they worked. Specifically, we'll be studying poets who doubled as editors, critics, publishers, as well as canon- and scene-makers. title: Gateway: Introduction to Critical Methods
description: Spring 2007 Interpreting Visual Culture This course assumes that notions of sight and seeing are fundamental to our conceptions of knowledge, power, and fantasies. The optical lens, for instance, records and projects images that help us construct spatial and temporal realities that in turn, determine our place as social subjects. Introducing different theoretical paradigms, the course will examine how knowledge and sight forge a tight alliance in our own visual terrain. The course centers on close readings and interpretation of several texts: magazines, advertisements, photographs, and films. Course assignments include several short papers and a final research paper.

title: Gateway: Introduction to Cultural Studies
description: Spring 2007 Reading Culture: Word and Image This course will focus on the relationship between words and images and the ways in which this relationship is critiqued in the field of Cultural Studies. A brief history of this field of inquiry and the theories and practices comprising it will be examined in pursuit of an understanding of the consumption of meaning which is the byproduct of our encounters with cultural productions, including popular culture and avant garde art. In order to pursue critical analyses of our own in the class, we will need to interrogate concepts such as culture, ideology, representation, taste, value, canon, style and subculture, and to pay particular attention to language, and the specific tensions between verbal and visual language. In order to ascertain what Roland Barthes called âthe doors of language,â we will study works from the realms of literature, film, photography, video and advertising, as well as theoretical and fictive readings. The overall frame around any of our inquiries in this course is the development of critical thinking and writing skills.

title: Gateway: Introduction to Cultural Studies
description: Spring 2007 Reading Culture: Word and Image MW 1:15-2:30 and 2:40-3:55 This course will focus on the relationship between words and images and the ways in which this relationship is critiqued in the field of Cultural Studies. A brief history of this field of inquiry and the theories and practices comprising it will be examined in pursuit of an understanding of the consumption of meaning which is the byproduct of our encounters with cultural productions, including popular culture and avant garde art. In order to pursue critical analyses of our own in the class, we will need to interrogate concepts such as culture, ideology, representation, taste, value, canon, style and subculture, and to pay particular attention to language, and the specific tensions between verbal and visual language. In order to ascertain what Roland Barthes called âthe doors of language,â we will study works from the realms of literature, film, photography, video and advertising, as well as theoretical and fictive readings. The overall frame around any of our inquiries in this course is the development of critical thinking and writing skills.

title: Gateway: Introduction to Cultural Studies
description: Spring 2007 This course is an introduction to the main theories and methods of Western literary and cultural analysis in the twentieth century. We will discuss why and how to think theoretically, and why critical cultural analysis is important. A focus in this introductory seminar will be on practical application of theoretical models to cultural texts. Topics will include Marxism, feminism, psychoanalysis, structuralism, postmodernism, deconstruction, critical media and cultural studies, among others. We will place special emphasis on the relevance of Western theory to the understanding of non-Western texts.

title: Gateway: Introduction to Critical Methods
description: Spring 2007 Intro to Spatial Analysis How can we understand the many cultural and personal narratives that spaces tell? This course examines the competing claims over design, usage, and representation of both real and imagined spaces in a primarily British context. Rather than attempt a comprehensive historical survey, we will focus on key tropes that appear repeatedly in literary and visual representations: the English country house, the cosmopolitan city, and the boundary between private and public. This interdisciplinary course will consider not only literary works, but also historical documents such as maps, architectural plans, and landscapes that give us a better understanding of how cultures assign meanings to spaces. Benefiting from the insights of critical theorists such as Henri Lefebvre, Raymond Williams, Michel de Certeau, and Beatriz Colomina, we will discuss how issues like subjectivity, class, economics, gender, and urbanization impact the creation of spatial rhetoric. Literary texts may include Shakespeareâs Hamlet, Ben Jonsonâs âTo Penshurst,â Andrew Marvellâs âUpon Appleton House,â Richard Sheridanâs School for Scandal, George Bernard Shawâs Heartbreak House, Agatha Christieâs The Murder of Roger Ackroyd, and Kazuo Ishiguroâs The Remains of the Day.

title: Gateway: Introduction to Critical Methods
description: Course description coming soon

title: Gateway: Introduction to Critical Methods
description: This course will introduce prospective English majors to the concepts of critical theory with a focus on narrative and rhetorical criticism. The goals of the course will be to develop a solid foundation in critical theory and to gain practice applying many of the concepts of critical theory to fictional narratives. The mode of the course will be the close reading of both critical texts and fictional narratives. At the end of this course, students will have a better understanding about "how" narratives develop progression, construct meaning, and produce effects for their readers. Critical texts for the course will include works by H. Porter Abbott, Roland Barthes, Jonathan Culler, Gerard Genette, Peter Rabinowitz, and James Phelan. Fictional narratives will be selected from the works of writers such as Katherine Anne Porter, James Joyce, Margaret Atwood, Jorge Luis Borges, Umberto Eco, John Fowles and others.

title: Medieval British Literature
description: This course will involve readings in medieval English literature, both in translation and in the original Middle English. Its readings will range from the Old English epic poem Beowulf, through such Middle English works as Sir Gawain and the Green Knight, numerous lyric poems, and selections from Chaucer's Canterbury Tales, and including as well works reflecting late medieval religious practices, Julian of Norwich's Revelations, the Cloud of Unknowing, and the Book of Margery Kempe. It will include three papers and a final exam, and there will be a creative option offered for two of the papers.

title: Medieval Performance
description: In this course we will explore various modes of performance in late medieval England in light of both medieval and contemporary theories of performance, ritual, and theater. Drama itself will be a significant focus; in particular, we will seek to gain an in-depth understanding of the great Corpus Christi cycles, examining their origins, the theories of âplayâ that motivate them, their robust humor and its relation to their theological aims, their function in both supporting and contesting ideals of civic unity, the ways they seek to construct and channel emotional response, and their tendency to collapse distinctions between spectator and spectacle, elite and popular, sacred and profane. But an equally important aim of the course is to investigate the performative aspects of late-medieval English culture more generally. Thus we will consider topics such as the oral performance of lyrics, romances, and sermons; the performativity of legal language and the language of Christian ritual; participatory affective piety, including the use of holy dolls, meditative writings, and devotional objects; civic processions and public executions; dance, gesture, and body language in both sacred and secular contexts; gender performance, including cross-dressing; and conduct books and other scripts written for the performance of everyday life. Prerequisite: a genuine willingness to learn Middle English. Since all primary texts will be in the original, an initial introduction to Middle English will be followed by weekly language exercises -- both written and oral. Students can expect to emerge as adept readers and performers of this rich and musical ancestor of our common tongue.

title: Milton and His Century Part One
description: Please note: This is a two-semester course, but students may elect to take only one semester for three credits Beginning with the /Ode on the Morning of Christ's Nativity,/ when he attained poetic majority at age twenty-one, and concluding with /Samson Agon/istess, written in old age, we will read over the course of the year more of Milton's poetry and prose than could be covered in a single semester. The course seeks to understand Miltonâs major poetry and prose both in themselves and in relation to various texts which reflect the experience and culture of Britain before, during, and after the Civil War. In examining the political, religious, philosophical, and cultural contexts, we will read Milton along with the work of some of his contemporariesâfor example, on the topic of religious liberty, Roger Williamsâs /The Bloudy Tenent of Persecution /(1644), excerpts from the Putney Debates, selections from A. S. P. Woodhouseâs collection /Puritanism and Liberty/, and Fredric Jamesonâs essay âReligion and Ideology.â Formal papers plus occasional informal writing exercises on the day's reading.

title: Milton and His Century Part Two
description: Beginning with the /Ode on the Morning of Christ's Nativity,/ when he attained poetic majority at age twenty-one, and concluding with /Samson Agon/istess, written in old age, we will read over the course of the year more of Milton's poetry and prose than could be covered in a single semester. The course seeks to understand Miltonâs major poetry and prose both in themselves and in relation to various texts which reflect the experience and culture of Britain before, during, and after the Civil War. In examining the political, religious, philosophical, and cultural contexts, we will read Milton along with the work of some of his contemporariesâfor example, on the topic of religious liberty, Roger Williamsâs /The Bloudy Tenent of Persecution /(1644), excerpts from the Putney Debates, selections from A. S. P. Woodhouseâs collection /Puritanism and Liberty/, and Fredric Jamesonâs essay âReligion and Ideology.â Formal papers plus occasional informal writing exercises on the day's reading.

title: Shakespeare
description: To quote Hamlet, "the play's the thing." Why has this small-town, middle-class man, with no college education, not only succeeded in his own era as an actor/playwright for a popular audience that included every sector of society, but also transcended four centuries to become a literary giant in British and American cultures and whose reputation has circled the globe? This course cultivates an understanding of Shakespeare's skill as a poet-dramatist and introduces one to the range and caliber of Shakespeare's plays in the three genres in which he wrote--comedy, history, and tragedy--spanning his approximately twenty-three years as a professional playwright in Elizabethan London. Six well-known plays from different stages of his career provide representative examples of his work: King Lear, The Merchant of Venice, Henry IV: Part One, Hamlet, Othello, and The Tempest. We will see King Lear in the theater and take advantage of the multiple opportunites of the "Shakespeare in Washington" performances that will be offered this spring. Both lecture and discussion methods will be employed with a strong emphasis on _informed_ discussion. Reflecting on Shakespeare's versatility and artistic habits, we will explore various aspects of Shakespearean dramaturgy--characterization, theme, dramatic structure, language, and plot--as well as sources and influences on his work. We will also engage intellectual contexts from various disciplines, such as history, philosophy, theology, psychology, art, and music, to understand better the attitudes and beliefs available to Shakespeare and his audiences. The main emphasis will be on the plays themselves as texts for performance in the public theater as well as the private "theater" of one's imagination.

title: Shakespeare
description: The course will focus on Shakespeareâs plays in performance. It has been designed to capitalize on the various productions of Shakespeare that will on stage during the Celebration of Shakespeare in Washington in the first half of 2007. It will discuss six plays by Shakespeare: Richard 3, Othello, King Lear, Coriolanus, As You Like It, and A Midsummer Nightâs Dream. Students will be expected to attend three productions of Shakespeare during the semester: Richard 3 at The Shakespeare Theatre, A Midsummer Nightâs Dream by the American Shakespeare Center on campus, and Coriolanus by the Royal Shakespeare Company at The Kennedy Center. The performances have been scheduled for three Tuesday nights (February 13, March 20, and April 17), and those who enroll in the course should have those three Tuesday evenings free to attend them. Actors and directors will also visit the class to discuss their work in these productions, and students will be asked to take part in several acting exercise during the semester to enhance further their understanding of Shakespeareâs plays in performance. Students who enroll in the course will be charged a fee of $90.00 to cover the cost of the tickets to Richard 3 and Coriolanus.

title: Shakespeare
description: In Shakespeareâs England, what would seem to us fundamentally âprivateâ matters were often a âpublicâ affair. Sexual desire, relations between parent and child, even the content of oneâs dreams â each was understood as having potentially powerful implications for social order at the village, civic or state levels. This course will consider public effects of private matters in Shakespeareâs poems and plays, with emphasis on topics such as subjectivity, romantic love, marriage, hospitality, household disorder, madness, and even forgetfulness. We will not only examine works in their historical contexts, but will also attend closely to literary and formal topics such as language and genre. Quizzes, reading responses, two papers and a final exam.

title: Shakespeare Replayed
description: As a cultural icon, Shakespeare has often been subjected to adaptation and appropriation by artists working in numerous genres. Not many of these latter-day Bards, however, respond to Shakespeare using his own dramatic idiom, invading his home turf of the playhouse to mount a radical, often controversial critique. This course thus pairs classic Shakespearean plays with provocative reworkings by contemporary playwrights, using comparative analysis and dramatic interpretation to examine changing notions of gender, class, race, empire, power, and history. By exploring how contemporary playwrights restage (or upstage) Shakespeareâs master narratives, we will open up larger issues concerning the politics of genre: can a transgressive viewpoint be adequately housed within a traditional literary structure? In post-colonial terms, can the key to the masterâs house be found within the masterâs architectonics? Using well-known texts such as Hamlet, Othello, and The Tempest as a springboard, we will consider how these canonical works play out against such upstarts as Suleyman al-Hassanâs The Al-Hamlet Summit, Tom Stoppardâs Rosencrantz and Guildensten are Dead, Ann-Marie MacDonaldâs Goodnight Desdemona (Good Morning Juliet), Timothy Findleyâs Elizabeth Rex, and AimÃ© CÃ©saireâs A Tempest.

title: Shakespeare and His Contemporaries
description: This course seeks to situate a small handful of Shakespeareâs works in relation to texts produced by his contemporaries. We will focus on pairings (and one âtriplingâ) of works that take up similar issues or genres; themes and texts might include anti-Semitism in The Merchant of Venice and Marloweâs Jew of Malta; sexuality, service and domesticity in Othello and the anonymous Arden of Faversham; and memory and revenge in Hamlet, Kydâs The Spanish Tragedy and Middletonâs The Revengerâs Tragedy. We will seek both to locate Shakespeare within his own literary culture and to begin to identify what is distinctive about his achievements. Requirements will include reading response essays, quizzes, regular attendance and participation, and papers.

title: Jews and Muslims in Early Modern Drama
description: Early modern England experienced substantial religious upheavals in its departure from the Catholic Church and establishment of the Protestant Church of England. In their struggle to define a new religious identity, the English often explored the ways in which they differed not only from other Christians but also non-Christians. In this course we will read sixteenth- and seventeenth-century English plays which represent some of these concerns in their accounts of two non-Christian religious traditions: Judaism and Islam. We will consider the significance of these representations both to discover what the English thought about religious difference as well as what these thoughts reveal about English culture. We will also attend to the ways in which representations of religious difference intersect with early modern ideas of race and gender.

title: Tutorial: Medieval/Renaissance Literature & Culture
description: Arranged by the student with an English department faculty member. See department for approval and section number. Fall and Spring.

title: 18th-Century British and American Lit
description: The eighteenth century had to deal with the shock of the new. The discoveries of the "New Science," as it was called, opened up previously unimaginable new worlds by means of the telescope and microscope, and the theories of Newton initiated a thorough rethinking of the nature of the physical (and therefore spiritual) universe; the colonization of the New World brought different cultures -- African, Amerindian, and European -- into violent collision and forced many thinkers to struggle with the complexities of cultural relativism; and new forces of economic and social change redefined the roles of both men and women and upset traditional notions of hierarchy and class. In this class, we will examine the ways many people responded to this sometimes exhilarating, sometimes frightening onslaught of modernity. Readings include /Oroonoko/; Rowlandsonâs account of her captivity by the Narragansetts; Swift's /Gulliverâs Travels/; /Moll Flanders/; poems by Alexander Pope; the autobiographies of Olaudah Equiano and Benjamin Franklin; Brown; /Edgar Huntly/; and Austen's /Emma/. There will be several substantive papers and a final exam.

title: Johnson and His Circle
description: A study of the major writings of Samuel Johnson and his circle of literary friends, this course examines in addition to Johnson=s work the remarkably varied forms of writing in England in the period 1740 to 1800. Attention will be given to the poetry, essays, novels, biographies, memoirs and letters, and to the moral, philosophical, historical, and critical writings of authors such as James Boswell, Edmund Burke, Charles Burney, Frances Burney, Lord Chesterfield, Edward Gibbon, Oliver Goldsmith, Thomas Gray, Sir Joshua Reynolds, Hester Thrale, and Horace Walpole.

title: Colonial Rise of the Novel
description: In The Colonial Rise of the Novel, Firdous Azim states, âthe novel is an imperialist project based on the forceful eradication and obliteration of the Otherâ (37). This course will explore the implications of this statement as it relates to eighteenth-century British novelists of both genders and the âOthersâ described in their texts. We will consider the validity of Azimâs claim as well as excavate the origins of the modern novel form by using a very specific thematic lens: colonialism. Since a significant number of the canonical and non-canonical texts that are associated with the origins and rise of the novel frequently employ the colonial Otherâboth as geographic space and narrative individualâwe will approach the novelâs development as a form that went hand-in-hand with Britainâs plan for colonial expansion. The novel, perhaps, provided Britons with justification and inspiration for this plan. To explore these ends we will consider eighteenth-century British novelistsâ obsessions with colonialismâobsessions that, curiously, begin and reach their height in novels written by British women. Readings will include some of the following: Aphra Behnâs Oroonoko, Daniel Defoeâs Robinson Crusoe, Jonathan Swiftâs Gulliverâs Travels, William Beckfordâs Vathek, Jane Austenâs Mansfield Park, Charlotte Bronteâs Jane Eyre, Anonymous The Woman of Colour, Samuel Johnsonâs The History of Rasselas, Unca Eliza Winkfield The Female American as well as critical essays by F.R. Leavis, Ian Watt, Michael Mckeon, Edward Said, Firdous Azim, Joseph Lew and Gayatri Spivak.

title: Literature in the Age of Revolution
description: We will read a series of significant books from the first half of the nineteenth century considering them within their historical context. The books: Blake Songs of Innocence and Experience; Wordsworth and Coleridge Lyrical Ballads; Byron: The Giaour, The Bride of Abydos, Laura; Austen: Pride and Prejudice; Tennyson: some early narrative poems and Maude; E. Bronte: Wuthering Heights; Whitman: Leaves of Grass (1855); Thoreau Walden. We will be using E. J. Hobsbawmâs The Age of Revolution for background and as we consider each writer we will also study the work of a visual artist who was that writerâs contemporary. Students will write a series of short essays in analysis and at the semesterâs end a retrospective commentary on âtheirâ Age of Revolution.

title: American Top Ten
description: This course will read and study in close detail some of the âclassicsâ of the American literary tradition (particularly, although not exclusively, those of the 19th century). We will consider these texts in their political and social histories; we will examine the aesthetic judgments made about them. This class will address the questions: How does one define âclassicâ? What makes a text great? What is the relation between a textâs reputed greatness and the culture in which it exists? Who gets to make these decisions? Once made can these judgments ever be reconsidered? More generally, why should these (or any) texts be considered especially âAmericanâ? Why ought we to read them? Texts: Hawthorne, The Scarlet Letter, Edwards, Selected Sermons, Poe, âPit and the Pendulum,â âWilliam Wilson,â Frederick Douglas, My Life and Bondage, Harriet Beecher Stowe, Uncle Tomâs Cabin, Mark Twain, Huckleberry Finn, Dixon, The Clansman, Baum, The Wizard of Oz, Fitzgerald, The Great Gatsby, Hurston, Their Eyes were Watching God. Mid-term, final; weekly written assignments.

title: American Women Critics
description: This course investigates how female performers of various kindsâballad singers, parlor poets, lecturers, pseudonymous autobiographers and playwrightsâshaped notions of womanhood, race, sexuality and "American-ness" in the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries in New England. These performers may be regarded as âcriticsâ because they all, in disparate ways, passed judgments on American culture; furthermore, they did not, as twenty-first century scholars do, worry about genre distinctions. Instead, they moved from one creative realm to another with ease. We will study the varied and complicated strategies that these critics used to gain access to the public sphere and to redefine the seemingly private sphere. For example, to protect their status as respectable wives and mothers, many women voiced their concerns indirectly, composing ballads and poems rife with hidden meanings, instead of publishing non-fiction commentaries. Others published their work anonymously, or under a male or female pseudonym. Still others populated the stage with dramatic characters who were bolder than they themselves could be. We will analyze the implications of the womenâs various avenues toward publication as well as their actual songs, poems, lectures, plays, and autobiographical novels. Our readings will include works by black and white women critics: Lucy Terry Prince, Phillis Wheatley Peters, Susanna Haswell Rowson, Judith Sargent Murray, Maria W. Stewart, Mary Carr Clarke, Louisa Medina, and Charlotte Sanford Barnes, Anna Cora Mowatt, and Harriet E. Wilson. We will analyze these womenâs publications within the context of contemporaneous debates about race and nation in an effort to understand the relationship between the performativity of identity and access to civic debates and full human rights.

title: Staging Anti-Slavery
description: In this course, weâll examine why and how late eighteenth and early nineteenth-century women staged their anti-slavery activism, in hopes of understanding how concepts of nationhood, race, and religion became entangled during the formative years of the United States. Weâll study the relationship between self-love, empathy, and activism. What propels women to enter the risky world of abolitionism, and how do they protect themselves as they enter fiercely contested public debates? Weâll track the changes in how abolitionists gained access to the public sphere or redefined the âprivateâ sphere from 1773 to 1865. To ground our analyses of anti-slavery activities, weâll search out archival materials at the Library of Congress, in Georgetownâs Early American Imprint collection, and in various databases. Weâll analyze womenâs various avenues toward publication as well as their actual anti-slavery poems, essays, plays, speeches, short stories, letters, and autobiographical novels. Our readings and archival adventures will help us understand how female anti-slavery advocates shaped notions of American womanhood in ways that reverberate, for good and ill, to this day. Our readings will include Phillis Wheatley, Susanna Haswell Rowson, Maria W. Stewart, Angelina and Sarah Grimke, Lydia Maria Child, Frances Ellen Watkins Harper and a host of lesser-known anti-slavery lecturers and essayists.

title: Dickens
description: A close reading of three major novels by Charles Dickens: Little Dorrit (1855 â 1857), A Tale of Two Cities (1859), and Great Expectations (1860 â 1861) in which we will consider the writer in the context of his era and the practice of fiction in the mid-nineteenth century. Discussion will dominate class time with students reporting on their analytical, inventive, and library research projects. There will not be any exams as such, but there will be short, factual quizzes on the dayâs reading which will begin each class session.

title: 19th C. British Literature
description: This course will trace the major forms and thematic concerns of nineteenth-century poetry through a focus on the works of three major poets: John Keats, Christina Rossetti, and Gerard Manley Hopkins.
